corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: From our model design, it is easy to know that given input length l 0 , the output frame length after the last convolutional layer (but before any of the fully-connected layers) is l 6 = (l 0 -96)/27.
To facilitate our analysis, we present the relative errors in figure with respect to comparison models.
We obtained the AG's corpus of news article on the web .
We choose the 4 largest classes from this corpus to construct our dataset, using only the title and description fields.
We choose the 4 largest classes from this corpus to construct our dataset, using only the title and description fields.
This dataset is a combination of the SogouCA and SogouCS news corpora , containing in total 2,909,551 news articles in various topic channels.
This gives us a large corpus of news articles labeled with their categories.
Although this is a dataset in Chinese, we used pypinyin package combined with jieba Chinese segmentation system to produce Pinyin -a phonetic romanization of Chinese.
The models for English can then be applied to this dataset without change.
The DBpedia ontology dataset is constructed by picking 14 nonoverlapping classes from DBpedia 2014.
The fields we used for this dataset contain title and abstract of each Wikipedia article.
We constructed several largescale datasets to show that character-level convolutional networks could achieve state-of-the-art or competitive results.
The present paper has considerably more experimental results and a rewritten introduction.

We show that when trained on largescale datasets, deep ConvNets do not require the knowledge of words, in addition to the conclusion from previous research that ConvNets do not require the knowledge about the syntactic or semantic structure of a language.
For each dataset, the bag-of-words model is constructed by selecting 50,000 most frequent words from the training subset.
The bag-of-ngrams models are constructed by selecting the 500,000 most frequent n-grams (up to 5-grams) from the training subset for each dataset.
We also have an experimental model that uses k-means on word2vec learnt from the training subset of each dataset, and then use these learnt means as representatives of the clustered words.
Figure shows 12 random first-layer patches learnt by one of our character-level ConvNets for DBPedia dataset.

Traditional methods like n-grams TFIDF remain strong candidates for dataset of size up to several hundreds of thousands, and only until the dataset goes to the scale of several millions do we observe that character-level ConvNets start to do better.
On the other hand, how well our model performs in comparisons depends on many factors, such as dataset size, whether the texts are curated and choice of alphabet.
Dataset size forms a dichotomy between traditional and ConvNets models.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
